title: Non-spherical Poincaré series, cusp forms and L-functions for GL(3)
speaker: Jack Buttcane
abstract: The analytic theory of Poincaré series and Maass cusp forms and their L-functions for $SL(3,Z)$ has, so far, been limited to the spherical Maass forms, i.e. elements of a spectral basis for $L^2(SL(3,Z)\PSL(3,R)/SO(3,R))$. I will describe the Maass cusp forms of $L^2(SL(3,Z)\PSL(3,R))$ which are minimal with respect to the action of the Lie algebra and give a (relatively) simple method for constructing Kuznetsov-type trace formulas by considering Fourier coefficients of certain Poincaré series.

proof_stages.label: GL(3) notation and Poincaré-series setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes G=PSL(3,R), Γ=SL(3,Z), the Iwasawa/Bruhat pieces U,Y,V,W,K, their characters and K-types, and defines Poincaré series from functions transforming on the left by a nondegenerate U-character.

proof_stages.label: Bruhat expansion of Fourier coefficients
proof_stages.summary: Applying the refined Bruhat decomposition to Fourier coefficients of the Poincaré series separates a finite arithmetic side of generalized Kloosterman sums from an Archimedean integral kernel.

proof_stages.label: Non-spherical cuspidal spectrum with fixed K-type
proof_stages.summary: To handle non-right-K-invariant functions, the speaker introduces vector-valued cuspidal automorphic forms of a fixed Wigner K-type, square-integrability, vanishing degenerate Fourier coefficients, and Casimir eigenvalue equations parameterized by μ.

proof_stages.label: Minimal K-types via Lie-algebra lowering
proof_stages.summary: Raising and lowering operators between K-types are used to define minimal-weight forms, with equivalent criteria by orthogonality to lifts, annihilation by lowering operators, and a polynomial condition in the Casimir eigenvalues.

proof_stages.label: Whittaker model and minimal-weight Fourier expansions
proof_stages.summary: Shalika multiplicity one identifies Fourier coefficients with Jacquet-integral matrix Whittaker functions, classifies the possible minimal-weight spectral parameters, and packages vector-valued forms into matrix-valued automorphic forms.

proof_stages.label: Complete spectral expansion and non-spherical Kuznetsov identity
proof_stages.summary: The matrix-valued minimal-weight forms yield a cuspidal spectral expansion, and choosing the Poincaré-series seed as an inverse Whittaker transform turns the spectral side into Fourier-coefficient products against a chosen test function while the Bruhat side becomes explicit Kloosterman sums with kernels.

proof_stages.label: Subconvexity via amplified local fourth moments
proof_stages.summary: The Kuznetsov formula is applied to prove subconvex central-value bounds for GL(3) L-functions, using a localized amplified fourth moment, Hecke relations, Poisson summation, sharp Archimedean cutoffs, and Mellin-Barnes estimates.

proof_stages.label: Other arithmetic consequences of the trace formula
proof_stages.summary: In response to questions, the speaker notes that the same non-spherical Kuznetsov machinery also gives weighted Weyl laws, density estimates for exceptional Archimedean and finite-place parameters, and large-sieve-type estimates.

examples.name: SO(3) rotations and Wigner D-matrices
examples.note: The compact group K is made concrete by identifying SO(3) with rotations and recalling the familiar Wigner D-matrix representations, illustrating the K-type bookkeeping rather than producing the argument.
examples.engagement_reason: the SO(3) Wigner D-matrices are used concretely as K-types in the transformation law, projections, and matrix-valued Whittaker/Jacquet constructions

passes.reason: SO(3) is used as the maximal compact rotation group, with Wigner D-matrices giving its standard symmetric irreducible representation family.

examples.name: The spherical Poincaré-series case
examples.note: Right K-invariant test functions and the Bump-Friedberg-Goldfeld spherical theorem are used as the known baseline against which the non-spherical extension is framed.
examples.engagement_reason: the spherical Poincaré-series case is defined and cited as the Bump-Friedberg-Goldfeld case, but the talk does not specialize or compute that case separately
